chunk_summary: Kingella kingae is a key pathogen in infants and toddlers, requiring early ultrasound and empiric therapy with cefuroxime or ceftriaxone, alongside anti-staphylococcal agents, due to subtler presentations and diagnostic challenges.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of Kingella kingae infections in infants and toddlers.
Understand the role of ultrasound and molecular testing in diagnosing osteoarticular infections.
Identify appropriate empiric antibiotic regimens for suspected Kingella infections.
Appreciate the diagnostic challenges posed by mild inflammatory marker elevations in this age group.
key_facts: Kingella kingae becomes a significant pathogen in children from six months onward.
Empiric therapy often includes cefuroxime or ceftriaxone, with additional anti-staphylococcal agents as needed.
Ultrasound is recommended early for suspected joint effusions in this age group.
CRP and synovial cell counts may be only moderately elevated in Kingella infections.
In India, Kingella is rarely confirmed with standard cultures; molecular testing improves diagnostic yield.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: ‘Ultrasound should be used early when a joint effusion is suspected, and empiric therapy should cover both Staphylococcus aureus and age-specific fastidious organisms.’ (Source: Indian Academy of Pediatrics Consensus Statement)
document_key_topics: Kingella kingae as a pediatric pathogen
Empiric antibiotic therapy in young children
